denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This notice concerns a prior authorization request that was submitted to Aetna on your behalf. Following review, Aetna is unable to approve the requested service.

Plan policy requires that a less costly, clinically appropriate modality be used before the requested service is authorized. On the information available, the criteria for the requested service are not met, and a lower-cost alternative has not been shown to be inadequate. For these reasons, coverage cannot be approved for the requested service.

To pursue coverage, your provider may submit documentation establishing that the lower-cost alternative is inappropriate. Until a determination is made on such documentation, the service remains not covered.

You or your provider may request a copy of the criteria used in reaching this determination. If your provider wishes to discuss this decision, a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged by calling the number on your member ID card.

You have the right to appeal this decision. To file an appeal, you or your authorized representative must submit a written request within 180 days of the date of this letter. Determination date: May 13, 2026.

Sincerely,
Aetna Utilization Management

Additional plan language regarding experimental/investigational definitions, network status, and member cost-sharing may apply and is available upon request.
clinical_context: The patient is a 47-year-old man with chronic post-traumatic stress disorder following a serious motor-vehicle collision two years ago, with a CAPS-5 score of 41 and a PCL-5 of 52, plus persistent intrusive symptoms, hyperarousal, and avoidance that impair work and family functioning; an SSRI (sertraline) trial produced only partial response. The requested service is a course of individual eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR), a trauma-focused individual psychotherapy. Contrary to the insurer's demand for a lower-cost modality, he has already completed a lower-intensity group-based program and a self-guided digital module over the past year without meaningful symptom improvement; his treating psychologist documents that individual trauma-focused therapy is the appropriate next modality given his symptom severity and prior failure of group and self-guided options. The denial letter engaged with none of these facts: it named no diagnosis, no clinician, and no submitted records, reading as generic boilerplate applicable to any case. The letter also omitted the ERISA disclosures required in an adverse benefit determination, including the member's right to bring a civil action and the plan-document and claim-file rights, leaving the member without notice of those procedural protections.

matrix_coverage.reasoning: matrix_cell matches field-for-field (Aetna, Prior Authorization, behavioral_health, 41-55, M, modality_substitution). PTSD F43.12 is behavioral_health; individual EMDR realistic. Rationale enacts modality_substitution (cheaper modality first). Age 47 in band; not 71+, null employer_archetype fine.
evidence_quotes: "sub_tactic": "modality_substitution"
a less costly, clinically appropriate modality must be used before the requested individual trauma-focused therapy is authorized

scenario_realism.reasoning: Frustratingly realistic. EMDR is first-line for chronic PTSD; a 47M with CAPS-5 41 is coherent. Aetna requiring a least-costly modality before individual EMDR is real step-therapy/modality-substitution behavior. Exploitable gaps (ignores failed group/self-guided modalities; case-blind boilerplate; omitted ERISA disclosures on a self-funded plan) mirror real adverse-determination defects.
evidence_quotes: a less costly, clinically appropriate modality must be used before the requested individual trauma-focused therapy is authorized
generic boilerplate that names no diagnosis, no treating clinician, and no submitted clinical records, and it omits the ERISA disclosure rights legally required in an adverse benefit determination

insurer_voice.reasoning: Procedural cadence and names Aetna (and an Aetna medical director), but omits Aetna's signature tells (no CPB, no InterQual) and the least-costly-modality framing leans toward the Cigna step-therapy tell. Generic payer boilerplate with the name swapped in. Anchor 3 (expected for the case-blind algo_boilerplate flaw).
evidence_quotes: This notice concerns a prior authorization request that was submitted to Aetna on your behalf.
a peer-to-peer review with an Aetna medical director may be arranged
insurer_voice.improvement: Cite an Aetna CPB / InterQual and drop the step-therapy framing to read distinctively Aetna (note: case-blind boilerplate is the intended flaw).

denial_logic.reasoning: Cites a plausible least-costly-modality rule matching modality_substitution. Authentically shoddy: circular and burden-shifting ('criteria not met', 'lower-cost alternative has not been shown to be inadequate', provider must prove the alternative inappropriate) without naming criteria, threshold, or the specific cheaper modality. No hallucinated named guideline; clear appeal room.
evidence_quotes: Plan policy requires that a less costly, clinically appropriate modality be used before the requested service is authorized.
your provider may submit documentation establishing that the lower-cost alternative is inappropriate

clinical_realism.reasoning: Credible and trauma-specific: F43.12 chronic PTSD from an MVC; CAPS-5 41 (moderate-severe) and PCL-5 52 (well above threshold) are consistent; intrusive/hyperarousal/avoidance clusters correct; sertraline partial response is first-line; EMDR is a guideline-supported next step after group/self-guided failure. No invented drugs or impossible courses.

an SSRI (sertraline) trial produced only partial response

diagnosis_treatment_match.reasoning: EMDR is a well-established, guideline-recommended (APA, VA/DoD, WHO) trauma-focused psychotherapy for PTSD. Correct clinical category; unambiguous match.
evidence_quotes: Post-traumatic stress disorder, chronic (F43.12)
Course of individual eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR) therapy

No suicidal ideation/self-harm, child abuse, end-of-life, or gender-affirming-minor content. No incitement against insurers. Aetna employer commercial (ERISA), in scope. No banned topic.

individual eye movement desensitization and reprocessing (EMDR), a trauma-focused individual psychotherapy

appeal_difficulty.reasoning: Highly exploitable on multiple vectors: omitted ERISA adverse-determination disclosures (civil action, plan-document/claim-file) — a §503 violation; generic case-blind boilerplate; and a circular premise ('lower-cost alternative has not been shown to be inadequate') contradicted by documented failure of group and self-guided modalities plus partial SSRI response. EMDR documented as the appropriate next modality.
exploitable_weaknesses: Omitted ERISA adverse-benefit-determination disclosures (right to civil action, plan-document and claim-file rights), a procedural Section 503 violation
Generic boilerplate denial naming no diagnosis, clinician, or records, with circular reasoning despite documented failure of group and self-guided modalities
strong_defenses: The plan can point to a facially valid step-therapy/least-costly-modality policy, but it is rebutted by the documented record

he has already completed a lower-intensity group-based program and a self-guided digital module over the past year without meaningful symptom improvement
